g.data-narration: Welcome to Connected Components. This fundamental graph theory concept helps us identify clusters in networks and analyze connectivity.
A connected component is a maximal set of vertices where every pair has a path between them. It represents an isolated cluster in a graph.
g.text: A connected component of an undirected graph is a
maximal set of vertices such that there is a path
between every pair of vertices in the set.

g.data-narration: A connected graph has exactly one component (the entire graph). A disconnected graph has multiple separate components that cannot reach each other.
g.text: • Path exists between all vertex pairs

g.data-narration: Connected components are maximal - you cannot add more vertices while keeping the subgraph connected. Each component is self-contained.
g.text: A connected component is MAXIMAL - no additional vertex from the graph can be added
while keeping it connected. Each component is a standalone island!

g.data-narration: To find connected components, we use DFS repeatedly. Start from any unvisited vertex, explore all reachable vertices - that's one component!
g.text: Initialize visited[] to false for all vertices
for each vertex v in G:

g.data-narration: Let's trace the algorithm on this disconnected graph. We have three components: vertices 0-2, vertex 3 alone, and vertices 4-6.
Step 1: Start DFS from vertex 0. It visits 1 and 2. All reachable vertices form Component 1. Step 2: Vertex 3 is unvisited - new DFS, Component 2.
The algorithm runs in O(V + E) time. Each vertex is visited once, and each edge is examined once during DFS traversal.
g.text: Each vertex visited once × V vertices + Each edge examined once × E edges = O(V + E)

g.data-narration: For directed graphs, we need Strongly Connected Components (SCC). An SCC requires directed paths in BOTH directions between every pair of vertices.
g.text: An SCC is a maximal set where for every pair (u,v), there is a directed path
from u→v AND from v→u. This is stricter than undirected connectivity!

g.data-narration: Consider this directed graph. Vertices 0,1,2,3 form one SCC because they can all reach each other. Vertex 4 is a separate SCC.
Kosaraju's algorithm finds SCCs in three steps: 1) DFS on original graph to get finish order, 2) Transpose the graph, 3) DFS on transposed graph.
g.text: Step 1: DFS on G → store vertices by finish time (stack)
Step 2: Transpose graph Gᵀ (reverse all edges)
Step 3: Pop from stack, DFS on Gᵀ → each DFS = one SCC

g.data-narration: First DFS on original graph: Visit 0→1→2→3→0 (cycle), then 2 explores 4. Vertices finish in order: 3, 4, 2, 1, 0.
Step 2: Create the transpose graph by reversing all edge directions. This flips outgoing edges to incoming and vice versa.
g.text: Reverse all edges: if G has u→v, Gᵀ has v→u

g.data-narration: Step 3: Process vertices from stack (reverse finish order). Pop 0, DFS on transposed visits 0→3→2→1. That's SCC1! Pop 4 visits 4 alone - SCC2!
g.text: Pop from stack in LIFO order (reverse finish):

g.data-narration: The condensation graph collapses each SCC into one vertex. The result is always a DAG - no cycles exist between SCCs!
g.text: Collapse each SCC into one vertex → The condensation graph is always a DAG!

g.data-narration: Connected components have many real-world applications: network analysis, social clustering, image segmentation, and more!
Remember: Connected components are for undirected graphs, SCCs for directed. Both use O(V+E) time. The condensation graph is always a DAG.
g.text: Undirected: Connected Components | Single DFS/BFS | O(V+E)

g.data-narration: For exams: Emphasize 'maximal' in definitions. Count components by counting DFS initiations. Remember condensation graphs are always acyclic!
g.text: • Emphasize "MAXIMAL" in component definitions • Count components = number of DFS initiations
• Condensation graph is always a DAG • SCC: paths in BOTH directions
